coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical immunosensor using nanotriplex of graphene quantum dots, Fe3O4, and Ag nanoparticles for tuberculosis
coredata.dc:creator: Tufa, Lemma Teshome
Oh, Sangjin
Tran, Van Tan
Kim, Jeonghyo
Jeong, Ki-Jae
Park, Tae Jung
Kim, Hwa-Jung
Lee, Jaebeom
coredata.dc:description: A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is modified with a nanotriplex [i.e., three nanolayers consisting of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-coated Fe3O4@Ag core-shell nanostructure (Fe3O4@Ag/GQD)]. The nanotriplex is a sensitive electrochemical biosensor platform for detecting Mycobacterium tuberculosis antigen (culture filtrate protein; CFP-10). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) conjugated to an anti-CFP-10 antibody are used as a label for signal amplification. The nanotriplex-based sensing platform attains synergetic electrochemical performance by the three different roles of three nanomaterials: Fe3O4 increases the surface to volume ratio; Ag enhances electrical conductivity; and GQD for loading more of the anti-CFP-10 antibody onto the electrode. The electron transfer kinetics at the surface of the electrode is simulated by means of the Butler–Volmer model. A sandwich-type immunoassay results by immobilizing the first antibody on the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD-modified GCE and by incubating this system with the antigen and then with AuNPs conjugated with the second anti-CFP-10 antibody (Ab2−AuNPs). The AuNPs are quantified by exposing the immunocomplex to a potential of 1.3 V for 40 s and scanning by differential pulse voltammetry. The immunosensor shows a wide linear range (0.005–500 μg/mL) with a limit of detection (signal/noise = 3) reaching 0.33 ng/mL. The results suggest that the reliable and robust performance with high selectivity and simple operation may be extended to detection of other biomarkers of pathogenic bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical immunosensor
Mycobacterium tuberculosis
CFP-10
Nanotriplex composite
Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the nanotriplex-based sensing platform
Computational simulation of electron transfer (ET) kinetics in the nanotriplex
Preparation and characterization of the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex
Cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance characterization
Computer simulation assisted analysis of experimental results of voltammetry
Electrochemical surface areas of the electrode
EIS characterization of the electrochemical immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the as-synthesized NPs: TEM and SEM images of (a) Fe3O4@PEI NPs, (b) Fe3O4@Au NPs, (c) Fe3O4@Ag NPs, (d) Fe3O4@Ag/GQD NPs, (e) Size distributions of (a) to (d), AFM images and the corresponding cross-sectional profile indicated by the red line drawn in the topographic image of (f) GO and (g) GQDs and (h) Raman spectra of GO and GQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Electrochemical characterization and computational simulation. (a) Cyclic voltammograms and (b) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− for Fe3O4-modified, Fe3O4@Ag-modified, and Fe3O4@Ag/GQD-modified GCEs and insert is equivalent circuit fitting EIS, and the simulated voltammetric (c) and EIS (d) at different electron transfer rate constant.

EIS characterization: EIS of different modified electrodes within the frequency range of 10−1–106 Hz at a formal potential of 220 mV in 0.1 M KCl containing 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− as a redox probe: (I) for bare GCE, (I) step 1, (II) step 2, (IV) step 3, (V) step 4, and (VI) step 5 (See Scheme 1b for each step).

Calibration graph and selectivity. (a) DPV of the electrochemical biosensor for CFP-10 detection at concentrations between 0 and 500 μg/mL and (insert) a calibration plot of DPV peak current vs. log C (ng/mL) ranges between 0 and 500 μg/mL; and (b) DPV responses of immune sensors to 0.5 mg/mL CFP-10, 1.8 mg/mL HspX protein of Mtb (16 kDa), 3.57 mg/mL Ag85, and 2% BSA.

Synthesis of nanotriplex NPs and fabrication of immunosensor (a) An outline of preparation of the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD NP probe (b) Schematic representation of the fabrication process of Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex-based electrochemical Mtb biosensor. Step 1: Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE, Step 2: Ab1/Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE, step 3: BSA/Ab1/Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE, step 4: CFP-10/BSA/Ab1/Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE, and step 5: Ab2-AuNP/CFP 10/BSA/Ab1/Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE.
Recovery tests of CFP-10 in urine samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical immunosensor using nanotriplex of graphene quantum dots, Fe3O4, and Ag nanoparticles for tuberculosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano Fusion Technology and Cogno-Mechatronics Engineering, Pusan National University, Busan, 46241, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nano Fusion Technology and Cogno-Mechatronics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul, 06974, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology and Medical Science, College of Medicine, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 35015, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Microbiology and Medical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Chungnam National University, Daejeon, 34134, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is modified with a nanotriplex [i.e., three nanolayers consisting of graphene quantum dot (GQD)-coated Fe3O4@Ag core-shell nanostructure (Fe3O4@Ag/GQD)]. The nanotriplex is a sensitive electrochemical biosensor platform for detecting Mycobacterium tuberculosis antigen (culture filtrate protein; CFP-10). Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) conjugated to an anti-CFP-10 antibody are used as a label for signal amplification. The nanotriplex-based sensing platform attains synergetic electrochemical performance by the three different roles of three nanomaterials: Fe3O4 increases the surface to volume ratio; Ag enhances electrical conductivity; and GQD for loading more of the anti-CFP-10 antibody onto the electrode. The electron transfer kinetics at the surface of the electrode is simulated by means of the Butler–Volmer model. A sandwich-type immunoassay results by immobilizing the first antibody on the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD-modified GCE and by incubating this system with the antigen and then with AuNPs conjugated with the second anti-CFP-10 antibody (Ab2−AuNPs). The AuNPs are quantified by exposing the immunocomplex to a potential of 1.3 V for 40 s and scanning by differential pulse voltammetry. The immunosensor shows a wide linear range (0.005–500 μg/mL) with a limit of detection (signal/noise = 3) reaching 0.33 ng/mL. The results suggest that the reliable and robust performance with high selectivity and simple operation may be extended to detection of other biomarkers of pathogenic bacteria.

ce:section.ce:para: Tuberculosis (TB) is an airborne infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb), which usually affects lungs (pulmonary TB) as well as other sites (extrapulmonary TB) such as lymph nodes, abdomen, genitourinary tract, and bones [1]. TB is still one of the top 10 leading causes of death worldwide and caused more deaths than HIV/AIDS in 2015 [2]. Its detection and diagnosis depend on laboratory routine procedures that are either unreliable or take too long for conclusive results [3,4]. Methods such as the skin test and sputum smear microscopy lack specificity and selectivity [5,6]. Meanwhile, some recently developed techniques such as culture-based methods, imaging, antigen detection, and molecular tests (nucleic acid amplification) have comparable specificity and selectivity but their use is limited by the higher costs and longer duration of the assays [7]. These drawbacks underscore the urgent need to develop a fast, sensitive, accurate, reliable, and cost-effective technique for rapid detection of Mtb, necessary for control of TB.

Electrochemical techniques have been shown to hold promise for the development of a cost effective, rapid, and accurate biosensor relevant to the detection and quantification of numerous clinically relevant analytes [8]. This technique has several advantages over other approaches, e.g., robustness, portability, and ease of use for detection and diagnosis of diseases. More recently, various electrochemical Mtb sensors have been reported. An enzyme-free voltammetric immunosensor was developed for the Mycobacterium lipoarabinomannan (LAM) antibody on a disposable chip with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.3 ng/mL [9]. Other TB-related antigens, e.g., MPT64 [10] and Mtb DNA [7], have been employed for electrochemical detection of TB. Nonetheless, among the multiple TB biomarkers that have been tested for biosensing of TB, none of them has attracted as much research attention as CFP-10 for its excellent antibody response during TB infection. This antigen also plays a key role in Mtb virulence [11,12] and has been studied by different types of TB-immunosensing platforms for monitoring of Mtb [13–16]. Various techniques have been developed for detection of CFP-10 antigen. For example, a gold interdigital electrode (IDE) [17] and matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) [18] were used for detection of CFP-10. These diagnostic techniques are costly, and the instruments may not be available at locations where TB is endemic. Therefore, developing an inexpensive, portable, and simple electrochemical method with greater sensitivity and selectivity for detection of Mtb based on its antigen (CFP-10) is a primary goal of this work.

The objective of this study was to improve sensitivity and selectivity of Mtb biosensing (compare to the conventional electrochemical sensing tools described above) by means of a nanocomposite involving several types of nanoparticles (NPs) to take advantage of their synergetic electrochemical activity: (1) magnetite (Fe3O4), which has strong superparamagnetic property, high adsorption ability, low mass transfer resistance, a large surface area, low toxicity, simple preparation, and excellent water solubility [33]. Fe3O4 NPs were often used in electrochemical immunosensor for preconcentration and separation of magnetic particles labeled biomolecules from solution with the help of external magnetic field. Fe3O4 NP-based nanocomposites have found excellent applications especially in fabrication of highly sensitive nanoenzyme immunosensors because of their good biocompatibility and ability to act as a nanocatalyst for reduction of H2O2 [34]. In addition, magnetized Fe3O4 NPs were utilized for enhancement of mass transport in electrochemical systems [19]. The mass transport transfer in solution occurs by diffusion, migration due to charge differences and convection [20]. Applying magnetic field to magnetic NPs modified electrodes can avoid an external mechanical convection to speed up the transport of reactants and products at electrode surface, (2) silver nanoparticles (AgNPs), which help to prevent Fe3O4 NPs from aggregation and enhance electrical conductivity to facilitate transfer of electrons, and (3) graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which show excellent electrochemical activity because they are favorable electron donors and acceptors (owing to their large surface area and abundant functional group edge sites) and have the ability to capture numerous molecules of the first anti-CFP-10 antibody (Ab1), making them a suitable candidate for an electrode material [21–25]. GQDs are relatively nontoxic, ecofriendly, chemically stable, and easy to prepare and have excellent dispersity and solubility in aqueous and organic solvents [41,42]. Some nanocomposites of GQDs show high peroxidase-like catalytic activity toward the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 for signal amplification [42,43], and the Fe3O4@GQDs hybrids can serve as electroactive probes for electrochemical detection of avian leucosis virus subgroup J [44]. This nanotriplex modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as working electrode for electrochemical detection of CFP-10 antigen.

Furthermore, a computational simulation was carried out to explain kinetics of the nanotriplex-modified electrode because in-depth analysis of the electrode kinetics will improve the critical understanding of its analytical sensitivity as part of a nanocomplex structure. Additionally, the voltammetric responses for different surface areas of electrode were simulated using COMSOL software to account the influence of the surface area on peak current heights and peak potential shifts. Owing to the synergetic effects of the components' properties, the new nanocomposite showed improved conductivity and abundant functionality accelerating electron transfer and increasing the antibody-binding ability. Next, a sandwich-type immunoassay was devised by immobilizing antibody 1 (Ab1) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) conjugated with antibody 2 (Ab2−AuNPs) on the GCE modified with Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex. Careful quantification of AuNPs after an immunoreaction revealed a wide linear range (0.005–500 μg/mL) with a LOD (signal/noise = 3) reaching 0.33 ng/mL, resulting in reliable and robust performance with high selectivity toward CFP-10. It is possible that the newly developed nanotriplex sensing platform can be extended to the detection of other biomarkers of pathogenic bacteria.

Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), silver nitrate (AgNO3), gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), formaldehyde (37%), ammonia (28%), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, molecular weight [MW] 29,000), poly(ethyleneimine) (PEI, MW 25,000), sodium acetate trihydrate (C2H3NaO2·3H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), tetrakis (hydroxymethyl) phosphonium chloride (THPC) ((HOCH2)4PCl), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals (Seoul, South Korea). All the reagents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water for all solution preparations was prepared at Milli-Q grade (>18.2 mΩ cm−1).

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were captured on a Park System (XE7, Seongnam, Korea) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired by means of a JEM-2100F field emission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were captured using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM S-4700; Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman system (Ramboss 500i; DongWoo Optron Co., Gwangju, Korea). The surface potential and hydrodynamic diameter were measured on a zeta-sizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Wooster, UK). Cyclic voltammetry, chronoamperometry, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out by means of an Aviumstat electrochemical interface (Eindhoven, Netherlands). A conventional three-electrode system was adopted including a bare or nanotriplex-modified GCE (3-mm diameter) as a working electrode, a Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a platinum wire auxiliary electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the nanotriplex-based sensing platform

ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical nanotriplex sensor based on three nanomaterials, i.e., Fe3O4@Ag decorated with GQDs, was created by multistep synthesis techniques in water. Fe3O4@PEI NPs were synthesized by a solvothermal method as reported by Wang et al. (2013) [26]. AuNP seed solutions were synthesized as previously reported by Ji et al. (2015) [27]. Graphene oxide (GO) was prepared by the modified Hummers method [28] with a slight modification through oxidation of graphite and the GQD was synthesized from GO via the Sono–Fenton reaction as previously reported by Routh et al. (2013) [29] (see Supplementary materials for the detailed synthesis procedure for each crucial component). To prepare the trifunctional electrochemical probe (nanotriplex), a sophisticated and optimized sequence was devised as follows (Scheme 1 a). For the synthesis of Fe3O4@Au, 6 mg of Fe3O4@PEI was added to 20 mL of the gold seed solution, and the mixture was treated by ultra-sonication (VCX750, Sonics, Newtown, USA) for 1 h and washed five times to remove unbounded AuNPs. To coat Fe3O4@Au with a silver layer, the optimized amount of 4 mg of Fe3O4@Au was dissolved in 26 mL of a 0.25 mM AgNO3 aqueous solution containing 0.2 wt% PVP. Excess amounts of 37% formaldehyde (40 μL) and a 25% ammonia solution (40 μL) were then added in this order. The mixture was sonicated for 5 min and washed five times to remove excess PVP. Finally, to decorate the prepared Fe3O4@Ag with GQDs, Fe3O4@Ag NPs were positively modified with PEI by sonicating 2 mg Fe3O4@Ag in 3 mL of a PEI solution (5 mg/mL). The amino-modified Fe3O4@Ag NPs were dispersed in 10 mL of a 0.4 mg/mL GQD solution and treated with ultrasonication for 1.5 h. The final product was washed five times with distilled water.

AuNPs were prepared according to a previously reported procedure [6]. In a typical procedure, 100 mL of 1 mM chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was brought to a boil, 10 mL of 1% sodium citrate (38.8 mM) was then added to the flask, and the mixture was heated for another 10 min. The flask was allowed to cool and the contents were stirred for 30 min.

Prior to surface modification, the GCE was polished to a mirror finish with 0.3-μm and 0.05-μm aqueous alumina slurries (Al2O3) and sonicated in ethanol and deionized water successively to remove residual alumina. Then, the electrode was allowed to dry under nitrogen. The fabrication procedure is illustrated in Scheme 1b. Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE was prepared by drop casting 6 μL of Fe3O4@Ag/GQD onto the surface of the GCE and air drying. Next, 5 μL of 0.13 mg/mL first anti-CFP-10 antibody (Ab1) was placed on Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE and incubated at 4 °C overnight for saturated adsorption of Ab1. The Ab1 modified electrodes were washed with 50 μL of 1 M PBS to remove all unbound Ab1 and 2% BSA was applied to the Ab1/Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE surface for 1 h to block nonspecific binding sites. After a final wash of the electrode with PBS, the resulting immunosensor was stored at 4 °C prior to use.

To carry out the immunoreaction and electrochemical measurements, the resulting immunosensor was incubated for 1 h with a series of different concentrations of the antigen (CFP-10; 5 μL). After that, the immunosensor was incubated with a conjugate of AuNPs with the second anti-CFP-10 antibody (Ab2-AuNP) for 1 h and finally unbound AuNP-Ab2 was washed away with PBS. Electrochemical measurements were performed afterwards using a conventional three-electrode system. During the measurement, the immunocomplexes were electrochemically activated with 0.1 M HCl at an optimized pre-oxidation potential of 1.3 V for 40 s to oxidize Au to Au3+, which was then reduced to Au0 during the DPV measurement. DPV was recorded in 0.1 M HCl from a starting potential of 0.6 V to ending potential of 0 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s, pulse amplitude of 50 mV, step potential of 4 mV, and pulse time of 20 ms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational simulation of electron transfer (ET) kinetics in the nanotriplex
ce:section.ce:para:
To recognize the ET kinetics of the nanotriplex-modified electrodes, a computational simulation was performed in the COMSOL Multiphysics 5.2a software (COMSOL, Inc. Burlington, USA). The Butler-Volmer (BT) model was applied to describe it at the electrode–electrolyte interface. For a reversible one-electron ET reaction at the electrode surface, we have
(2)

e
−
⇋ R

The net rate of the electrochemical reaction is given by the BT model [30,31]:
(3)

The heterogeneous rate constants in the forward and backward reaction are as follows:

)

In equations (4) and (5), α is the transfer coefficient (0.5 in this experiment),
k
0
is the standard heterogeneous ET rate constant at the formal reversible potential
E
0 '
of the redox couple, F is the Faraday constant, R is the universal constant, T is the absolute temperature, and E is the potential applied to the electrode. In order to investigate the change in modification of the surface of electrodes by different NPs, three different surface areas have been considered for calculation of voltammetric and electrochemical impedance. The standard ET rate constant denotes electrochemical activities of electrodes for a given reaction. Therefore, when electrodes are modified with different NPs, distinct electrochemical properties of the modified surfaces can be determined from experimental values of
k
0
. The analysis of electrode kinetics benefits from information about the analytical sensitivity of the electrodes. For larger values of
k
0
, diffusion is the rate-determining step, whereas for a low value of
k
0
, ET is the rate-determining step. Chemical processes with large
k
0
value attain equilibrium in short time, but chemical processes with small standard rate constants are usually sluggish. Therefore, we investigated the influence of the changes in rate constant
k
0
on the cyclic voltammogram and Nyquist plot of the EIS by numerically solving the electroanalytical BT (equation (6)):

is overpotential; and n is the number electrons involved in the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex
ce:section.ce:para: The nanotriplex particles (Fe3O4@Ag/GQD) were prepared with different reagents by stepwise functionalization. The size and shape of the products were characterized by TEM and SEM. The TEM image and inset SEM image (Fig. 1 a) show the superparamagnetic PEI-coated Fe3O4 NP with a diameter of approximately 200 nm. The advantages of PEI functionalization were an increase in the dense adhesion of gold seed and prevention of agglomeration because of the excellent hydrophilic properties of the polymer [32]. Fig. S1 shows the magnetization hysteresis loop M versus H of the as-prepared porous Fe3O4 NPs measured at 300 K. The saturation magnetization of PEI coated Fe3O4 NPs is 69 emu·g−1. To fabricate the silver shell, the AuNP seeds adsorbed on the magnetic NPs were used as nucleation sites for uniform growth of the silver shell (Scheme 1a). The growth of Au seeds on the surface was characterized by the change in zeta potential from +35.3 to −21.5 mV (Fig. S2), and the TEM image in Fig. 1b shows the dense adhesion of AuNPs to the branched PEI-coated magnetic nanoparticle. Next, the Fe3O4@Ag composite was prepared by dispersing Fe3O4@Au in an aqueous solution of silver nitrate containing 0.2 wt% PVP and was reduced with an excess of formaldehyde and ammonia [35,36]. The TEM image (Fig. 1c) and inset SEM image revealed that Fe3O4@Ag NPs were successfully synthesized and had a continuous shell with nanoscale roughness. Fe3O4 NPs are often modified by AgNPs with a core shell structure to prevent Fe3O4 NP aggregation and to increase stability [37,38]. Fig. 1e presents size distribution of Fe3O4@PEI NPs, Fe3O4@AuNPs, Fe3O4@Ag NPs and Fe3O4@Ag/GQD NPs, with average size of 200, 205, 260 and 270 nm, respectively. A random distribution and spaces among quantum dot-coated Fe3O4@Ag core-shell nanostructures were observed in the TEM image where these proved in exhibiting a large surface area.

GQDs were synthesized from graphene oxide (GO) via the Sono–Fenton reaction (Fig. 1f). The AFM images in Fig. 1g show a uniform distribution of quantum dots formed during the synthesis step. The GQDs, which are zero-dimensional carbon materials with lateral dimensions less than 100 nm [23], possess unique electronic and optical properties arising from quantum confinement and edge effects that make them ideal for bio-imaging, optoelectronics, photochemical catalysis, drug delivery, and biosensor applications [39,40]. Our newly prepared GQDs have fluorescent properties (Fig. S3). The emission wavelength of GQDs is strongly excitation-dependent. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of GQDs is either due to the lateral size of GQDs in the sample or to different emission sites on GQDs [45]. The Raman spectra of GO and GQDs revealed the presence of “disorder” D (1348 cm−1) and crystalline G (1592 cm−1) bands corresponding to the disorder-induced phonon mode and Raman-allowed phonon mode of vibrations, respectively [46]. The signal strength of D and G bands depends strongly on the amount of disorder in graphitic carbon. The ratio of corresponding D to G bands (ID/IG) of the GQDs was 0.93. This is lower than the typical values of the ID/IG ratio observed in GO (1.13) owing to the reduction of GQDs by the Fenton reagent (Fig. 1h).

The Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplexes were synthesized by means of positively charged PEI. The amino-modified Fe3O4@Ag NPs were resuspended in a solution containing an excess of GQDs and subjected to ultrasonication. The TEM image in Fig. 1d confirms dense coverage (on the surface of the branched polymer) with GQDs, and this result was confirmed by EIS and cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 2 a and b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical redox probe ions [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− were chosen to characterize various modified electrodes. Fe3O4/GCE, Fe3O4@Ag/GCE, and Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE were tested as working electrodes. The cyclic voltammetry data on 10.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in a 0.1 M KCl solution are shown in Fig. 2a. The anodic peak current to the cathodic peak current ratio (iap/icp) was approximately equal to unity. The [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple showed excellent reversible redox behavior with Fe3O4-modified, Fe3O4@Ag-modified, or Fe3O4@Ag/GQD-modified GCEs. The small peaks observed at 0.12 V corresponds to the oxidation of Ag0 to Ag+, and the peak at −0.06 V corresponds to the reverse reduction of Ag ions. As compared with Fe3O4/GCE, Fe3O4@Ag/GCE yielded a greater oxidation and reduction peak current due to the high electrical conductivity and high surface area of Ag NPs. When Fe3O4 is combined with noble-metal NPs, the newly formed nanocomposite has a higher reaction activity than either of their components [47]. Similarly, to observe the effect of magnetic field on mass transport, cyclic voltammetry and chronoamperometric methods were used to measure electrochemical properties of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox couple using Fe3O4@Ag/GQD modified GCE in the presence and absence of external magnet. In presence of external magnet (380 mT), increases in currents were observed for both cyclic voltammetry and amperometry measurements (Fig. S4). The superparamagnetic, Fe3O4 NPs in nanotriplex allowed the generation of an ultra-high magnetic gradient near the electrode which facilitated the mass transport.

Fig. 2b shows the Nyquist diagrams of EIS for the above electrodes. The EIS was conducted as a function of frequency (0.1–100 kHz) at a polarization potential of 220 mV. In the Nyquist plot of EIS, generally the semicircle portion at higher frequencies corresponds to the ET-limited process, whereas the linear part at a lower frequency represents the diffusion-limited process, and the diameter of the semicircle in the higher-frequency region corresponds to the ET resistance (Ret). The Ivium software was employed for simulation of EIS results. The Randles equivalent circuit is shown in the inset of Fig. 2b. The electrochemical properties of electrode-solution interfaces including Faradic current and diffusion transport are commonly explained by Randles equivalent circuit [48]. The first part of a circuit, which is related to high-frequency domain, consists the electrolyte resistance (R1) associated with the bulk properties of the electrolyte solution. The second part, for intermediate frequency, contains parallel resistance (R2) and capacitor (C1) of this layer was related to the electrode-film interfaces while the last parallel consists of a charge transfer resistance (R3) and double layer capacitance (C2) was ascribed the film-solution interfaces.

At the Fe3O4/GCE, the redox process of the probe showed the highest Ret owing to the insulating nature of Fe3O4. The Fe3O4@Ag/GCE showed an almost straight line, indicating that the Fe3O4@Ag NPs are excellent electrical conductors, yield high-intensity ET, and can facilitate the ET from the redox center of a material to the surface of the electrode [49]. The Ret value of the probe with Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE was slightly higher, suggesting that the ET between the redox probe and electrode surface was slightly hindered by oxygen-containing functional groups on the GQDs. GQD–metal nanocomposites have special properties that are not observed in bulk metal or metal oxides alone [50–52]. For example, GQD–metal oxide composites keep inter planar spacing of the graphene sheets from aggregation and provide improved ET [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computer simulation assisted analysis of experimental results of voltammetry
ce:section.ce:para:
Numerical calculations of voltammetric and electrochemical impedance of [Fe(CN)6]3- at different physicochemical parameters (such as scan rate, heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant) and surface area of electrodes were performed. Computer-simulated voltammograms depending on the heterogeneous ET rate constant are shown in Fig. S5a; different curves were obtained for different
k
0
values (ranging from 5.1 × 10−9 to 5.1 × 100 m/s). In the resulting voltammograms, an overpotential developed, and the peak potential for the oxidation reaction shifted to more positive values as the electrochemical rate constant decreased. In addition, as the values of
k
0
decreased, the peak current, i p, became constant. These trends are characteristic of the irreversible limit according to the Randles–Sevick equation [54]:
(7)
i
p
i r r
= 2.99 ×
10
5
(
n
'
+ α )
1 / 2
n A C
D
1 / 2
v
1 / 2
where
i
p
is the peak current in amperes,
n
'
is the number of electrons transferred per mole before the rate-determining step, D is the diffusion coefficient, C is concentration, v is the scan rate, and A is the surface area of an electrode. The possibility of fitting the experimental voltammogram results to theory by means of kinetic parameters,
E
0 '
,
k
0
, and α is uncertain. At a higher value of
k
0
, the voltammogram shape is weakly dependent on the kinetic parameters, and greater uncertainty in
k
0
and α was obtained from this and similar voltammograms. Fig. 2c shows simulated CV curves referred to each modified electrode with different electron transfer rate constant (k0 1 = 5 × 10−6, k0 3 = 5 × 10−5, and k0 2 = 5 × 10−4 m/s for black, blue and red respectively). GCE modified with Fe3O4@Ag NPs, showed the potential shift and oxidation and reduction peak enchantment. As shown in Fig. 2a, after modifying electrode by Fe3O4@Ag NPs, oxidation and reduction potential are shifted and the oxidation and reduction current is increased. Similarly, from the simulation result, when electron transfer coefficient is increased, peak potential shift and peak oxidation and reduction current increased in the same pattern. Electrode modified with highest conductive Fe3O4@Ag NPs increased the electron transfer processes.

To understand the electrochemical activities and ET mechanism of the nanotriplex-based electrode, the BT model was applied to describe EIS responses. A frequency ranges from 10−1–105 Hz was simulated at room temperature (25 °C) with sinusoidal input voltage of 220 mV, and the resulting Nyquist plot is shown in Fig. S5b. The EIS results revealed a large impedance increase at all frequencies, especially at lower
k
0
, according to the semicircle in Fig. 2d and Fig. S5b. The simulation data tell us that if the increasing heterogeneous rate constant results in a significant increase in peak current and a shift in potential, then these phenomena must in part indicate that modification of the electrode surface with different NPs changes surface chemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical surface areas of the electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
To measure the electrochemically active surface areas of bare and modified electrodes, cyclic voltammetry was performed on a 10 mM [Fe(CN)6]3- redox probe as shown in Fig. S6. The electrochemical surface area was calculated using the reversible Randles–Sevick equation [20] (Equation (8)):
(8)
i
p
r e v
= 2.686 ×
10
5
n
3 / 2
A C
D
1 / 2
v
1 / 2

The electrochemical active surface areas (A) of electrodes calculated from the slope of the peak current (
i
p
) versus the square root rate at T = 25 °C according to the above equation were 0.33 ± 0.005, 0.21 ± 0.07, and 0.11 ± 0.06 cm2 for Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE, Fe3O4@Ag-modified GCE, and bare GCE, respectively. Magnetic NPs are preferably used as a core part of the probe in electrochemical immunosensors because of their characteristic property of easy magnetic separation and large surface area allow the binding of much amounts of biomolecules as well as a decrease of the matrix interference [55]. Using this magnetic NPs as a core, we further coated Ag and GQD to produce conductive large surface area nanotriplex. Finally, it could be used for decorating GCE for sensors. The surface areas of Fe3O4@Ag/GCE and Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE were larger than that of bare GCE. Thus, the nanotriplex composite including Fe3O4, Ag, and GQDs increases the surface area of the bare electrode, and this effect is directly related to the sensitivity of electrochemical analysis.

Fig. S7 shows the voltammograms simulated for different surface areas of the electrode. The peak heights and the potential shifts were observed using Butler–Volmer model. Both reduction and oxidation of ferro/ferric redox couple peak heights and peak potentials show variation as the radius of the electrodes change from 1.5 to 3.5 mm. The peak intensity increases with increasing k0 or surface area of an electrode. When k0 or surface areas increases, oxidation, and reduction peak positions were moved toward the center and when k0 or surface area decreases, those peaks position were moved outward center. The electrochemical activities of electrodes modified with nanotriplex were enhanced because of two reasons; The first is conductive materials such as silver and GQD are facilitating the electron transfer between the solution and surface of electrodes. The second is a change of surface area of an electrode surface due to nanoparticle's increases the electrochemical reaction. As shown in Fig. 2a, the cathodic peak and anodic peaks were shifted due to the silver shell of magnetic NPs, which increases the electron transfer coefficient of the electrode surface. The differences of peak positions between Fe3O4@Ag/GCE and Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE were due to the increase of surface area to volume ratio of the electrode by GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS characterization of the electrochemical immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: EIS provides useful information regarding processes of surface modification of electrodes. Fig. 3 shows Nyquist plots of the EIS of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− for bare GCE (curve I), step 1 (curve II), step 2 (curve III), step 3 (curve IV), step 4 (curve V), and step 5 (curve VI) at a polarization potential of 220 mV and frequency range of 0.1–100,000 Hz (See Scheme 1b for each step of sample preparation). Their corresponding Ret values were 0.32, 0.30, 3.85, 4.34, 4.85, and 1.89 kΩ, respectively. After the bare GCE was modified with Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex, the resistance for the redox probe slightly decreased because of the high ET ability of the nanocomposite (curve II). The Ab1-modified electrode showed higher Ret (curve III) as compared to the bare GCE and Fe3O4@Ag/GQD/GCE. Ab1 acts as a barrier to the ET at the electrode interface. Formation of the Ab1/BSA-modified electrode further increases Ret (curve IV), indicating that the treatment with BSA could block nanocomposite sites that did not react with Ab1 to prevent nonspecific adsorption. When the Ab1/BSA-modified electrode was incubated with the analyte (CFP-10), the highest Ret value was observed in the curve IV owing to the blockage of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− process from accessing the CFP-10/BSA/Ab1 electrode surface effectively. In the presence of the AuNP-Ab2 conjugate, Ret then decreased (curve VI), proving that AuNPs promote electron transfer and enhance the conductivity of the electrode (Fig. S8). These results indicate successful stepwise binding of biomolecules on the surface of the immunosensor.

Under optimized conditions (10 mL of 0.1 M HCl, pre-oxidation potential of AuNP 1.3 V for 40 s, and 5 μL of CFP-10 at different concentrations), the peaks of current signal generated upon reduction of the Au nanoprobes strengthened with the increasing concentration of CFP-10, as shown in Fig. 4 a. An increase in the concentration of analyte CFP-10 during immunocomplex formation resulted in Au nanoprobes' binding to captured CFP-10; this process enhanced the DPV peak current. The amount of the captured label was proportional to the concentration of CFP-10. The calibration plot showed a good linear relation between the DPV peak current and the logarithm of CFP-10 concentration in the range of 5 ng/mL to 500 μg/mL with a correlation coefficient of 0.997 (insert of Fig. 4a). The LOD for CFP-10 calculated according to International Conference on Harmonisation (ICH) guidelines [56] (Equation (9)) was 0.33 ng/mL, and this low LOD may result from the synergetic electrochemical performance of the novel Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex.
(9)
L O D =
3.3
S
y / x
n g / m L
m
where Sy/x is standard error at y-intercept and m is a slope.

The synergetic combination of GQDs and nanomaterials was electrosensitive and selective for electrochemical detection of Mtb. Selectivity of the immunoassay was evaluated using the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex-modified GCE incubated with four different proteins: CFP-10 (which is the target protein), and other similar sizes and easily cross-linkable proteins such as antigen 85 complexes (Ag85), HspX protein of Mtb (16-kDa), and bovine serum albumin (BSA). DPV responses of the immunosensor to 0.5 mg/ml CFP-10, 3.57 mg/mL shows that the average peak current (Ip) was 6.5 μA. Meanwhile, the Ip of HspX, Ag85 and BSA with much higher concentration showed very low value in the range of 0.6–1.34 μA. These results indicated good specificity of the proposed immunosensor (Fig. 4b). The reproducibility of the proposed immunoassay was evaluated by means of relative standard deviation (RSD). A sample of 5 μg/mL CFP-10 was detected using the same immunosensor five times, and RSD of 4.2% was obtained, indicating acceptable reproducibility of the sensor. To validate the reliability of the proposed electrochemical immunosensor, the spiked samples were analyzed with the Fe3O4@Ag/GQD-modified GCE by DPV. Different concentrations of CFP-10 (0.005, 0.5, 5, and 50 μg/mL) were added into crude urine samples of healthy person, respectively. The results indicated that the CFP-10 recovery was between 93.76% and 106%, with RSD values ranging from 3.06% to 9.50% (Table 1 ). These data indicate that the sensor is suitable for practical applications.

We synthesized a core-shell Fe3O4@Ag/GQDs nanotriplex for electrochemical Mtb sensing. The nanotriplex was prepared from (1) Fe3O4, which ensures high surface energy, low mass transfer resistance and large surface area. (2) AgNPs, which help to prevent Fe3O4 NPs from aggregation and enhance electrical conductivity to facilitate ET, and (3) GQDs, which show excellent electrochemical activity owing to their large surface area and abundant functional group edge sites and have the ability to capture numerous molecules of the first anti-CFP-10 antibody. The effect of heterogeneous rate constant k0 on the ET kinetics at the surface of the electrode was simulated using the BT model. The resulting voltammogram and Nyquist plot revealed that when the electrochemical rate constant decreases, overpotential develops and Re increases. A sandwich electrochemical immunosensor was developed on the basis of Fe3O4@Ag/GQD nanotriplex for immobilization of Ab1 and by means of the Ab2–AuNPs conjugate as the label for the CFP-10 detection. The theoretical electrochemical active surface areas of the nanotriplex modified electrode calculated using the Randles–Sevick equation was 0.33 ± 0.005 cm2. The large surface area of the nanocomposites accelerates electron transfer and increases the antibody-binding ability. The current response of the electrochemical immunosensor was improved because of the synergetic effect of the nanocomposite. The resultant immunosensor shows good reproducibility, high selectivity, and a low LOD. The good recovery rates achieved on human urine samples spiked with CFP-10 indicate that the sensor is suitable for practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the Korean Health Technology R&D Project of the Ministry of Health & Welfare, Republic of Korea [HI16C1553], a National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) granted funded by the Korean Government (MSIP) [2016R1A2B4012072]. The sponsors played no role in study design; in the collection, analysis and interpretation of data; in the writing of the report; and in the decision to submit the article for publication.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2018.09.108.
